When I first started learning React, I used to create state inside the component where it was needed.
It worked perfectlyвҖҰ until two components needed access to the same data.
ThatвҖҷs where I discovered one of ReactвҖҷs most important design patterns: Lifting State Up.
LetвҖҷs understand it with a simple example.

The Scenario
Imagine we are building a small user management application.
We have two components:
App
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Form
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ UserCards
вҖў Form is responsible for collecting user information.

вҖў UserCards is responsible for displaying all submitted users.
Initially, I created the state inside the Form component.
function Form() {
const [users, setUsers] = useState([]); // ...
}Everything looked fine.
Until I realised somethingвҖҰ
How will UserCards access the users state?
It canвҖҷt.
Because the state belongs only to the Form component.

The Problem
App
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Form
в”Ӯ users вң…
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ UserCards вқҢSince UserCards is a sibling of Form, it has no direct access to the state stored inside Form.
This is where many React beginners get stuck.

The Solution: Lift the State Up
Instead of storing the state inside Form, move it to their nearest common parent.
App
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ users вң…
в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Form
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ UserCardsNow the parent component owns the state and simply shares it with its children using props.
function App() {
const [users, setUsers] = useState([]); return (
<>
<Form setUsers={setUsers} />
<UserCards users={users} />
</>
);
}Now the flow becomes much cleaner.
FormвҶ“setUsers()вҶ“App State UpdatesвҶ“App Re-rendersвҶ“Updated PropsвҶ“UserCards Re-renders
Why Is This Better?
Keeping shared state in the nearest common parent gives us:

вҖў A single source of truth

вҖў Better data flow

вҖў Easier debugging

вҖў Better component communication

вҖў Cleaner and more maintainable code
Instead of multiple components maintaining separate copies of the same data, they all rely on one shared state.

Final Thoughts
One of the biggest lessons I learned while working with React is this:
If multiple components need the same data, donвҖҷt duplicate the state вҖ” lift it up to their nearest common parent.
This simple design pattern makes your applications easier to understand, easier to maintain, and aligns perfectly with ReactвҖҷs one-way data flow philosophy.
Sometimes, writing better React isnвҖҷt about adding more code вҖ” itвҖҷs about placing your state in the right place.
Happy codingвқӨпёҸ
